sections.title: U.S. MLS FEEDS COMPLIANCE POLICY
sections.contentHtml:
Expert Edition for Marketplace Platforms

Corrected, Verified & Extended — March 16, 2026

Authoritative Reference for Feed Licensing, Display Rules, Privacy & Regional MLS Requirements

sections.title: National Framework and Regulatory Decentralization
sections.contentHtml:
The foundational structure of the U.S. MLS system has shifted from a rigid top-down mandate to a model of "unfettered local discretion" following a comprehensive risk assessment conducted in late 2025. Following recommendations from the MLS Policy Risk Assessment Presidential Advisory Group (PAG) in November 2025, eighteen major policy repeals and updates were approved to mitigate antitrust exposure and modernize operations for the 2026 calendar year.

MLS Count — Verified Figure

As of early 2026, RESO tracks just over 500 MLS systems in the United States. The National Association of REALTORS® (NAR) oversees approximately 500 REALTOR-affiliated MLSs. Any reference to "580" in prior versions of this document was incorrect and has been removed. Use 'just over 500' in all JSON metadata fields.

The 18 NAR MLS Policy Changes — Full Reference

On November 14–16, 2025, during NAR NXT in Houston, NAR's Executive Committee approved 18 comprehensive updates to the MLS Policy Handbook — the most extensive update in 20 years. These changes took effect January 2026. Five policy changes with direct platform impact were missing from the prior version of this document and are added here.

CRITICAL ADDITION — POLICY 7.87 REPEAL: NAR repealed Policy Statement 7.87 (Transmittal of Participants' Listings to Aggregators and Public Sites). There is NO longer any national framework governing whether an MLS must transmit listing data to third-party portals or aggregators. Syndication is now entirely a local MLS decision. Platforms cannot assume any national syndication right — every market now requires a per-MLS syndication agreement.

PolicyChange
Platform ImpactPS 7.7 — Association Membership as MLS Prerequisite
REPEALEDMembership may still be required locally. Platforms must verify participation rules per MLS — no national standard.
PS 7.58 — IDX Display Limited to REALTORS® OnlyAMENDED — local restriction removed
MLSs can no longer limit IDX display rights exclusively to REALTOR® members. Broader IDX eligibility is now the default.PS 7.87 — Syndication to Third-Party Aggregators
REPEALED — CRITICAL FOR PLATFORMSNo national syndication framework exists. Every MLS sets its own syndication rules. Platforms must execute individual agreements per MLS.
PS 7.62 — Open Listings Excluded from MLSREPEALED
MLSs may now choose to accept open listings where permitted by state law. Update listing status logic accordingly.PS 7.89 — MLS Financial Penalty Cap ($15,000)
REPEALEDThe national $15,000 MLS violation fine cap is removed. Local MLSs now set their own fine schedules with no ceiling.
Section 5 — MLS Disciplinary GuidelinesFULLY REPEALED
Entire disciplinary framework removed from national handbook. Each MLS now governs its own discipline, fines, and enforcement.PS 7.71 & 7.73 — Offer Presentation Rules
REPEALED from MLS rulesOffer obligations now governed solely by NAR Code of Ethics and state law — not enforceable as MLS violations.

NAR Self-Certification Deadline — New in 2026

Unless indicated otherwise in the NAR 2026 MLS Handbook, all local MLS organizations were required to self-certify adoption of the updated Handbook policies by March 1, 2026. This deadline is not present in prior versions of this document and is critical for compliance auditing: any MLS that has not self-certified by this date is operating out of policy and presents elevated feed risk for marketplace platforms.

Participation Models — Thompson Broker Rule (FL, GA, AL)

Since 1991, Florida, Georgia, and Alabama have permitted non-REALTOR member access to the MLS via what is commonly called the "Thompson Broker" rule. In these three states, licensed brokers who are not NAR members retain the right to access MLS data and IDX feeds. Marketplace platforms operating in these states must account for Thompson Broker participants in their feed access verification logic.

StateParticipation Rule
NAR Membership Required?Compliance Note
FloridaThompson Broker (1991)
NoLicensed brokers get MLS access regardless of NAR membership.
GeorgiaThompson Broker (1991)
NoSame as Florida; verify broker license only.
AlabamaThompson Broker (1991)
NoSame as Florida; verify broker license only.
All Other StatesStandard NAR / Local MLS Rules
Varies by MLSCheck local MLS participation policy.

Access Mechanisms

Marketplace platforms interact with listing data through three primary mechanisms, each fundamentally altered by the August 17, 2024, antitrust settlement and subsequent 2026 policy updates.

Mechanism2026 Compliance Focus
Mandatory GovernanceIDX
Public display; strict compensation data filtering.NAR Policy 7.58; Local MLS IDX Rules.
VOWGated consumer access; registration and terms of use.
NAR VOW Policy; State Privacy Law.Syndication
Broad third-party distribution; seller opt-out priority.Participant Agreements; NAR MLOS Policy.
BBO FeedInternal analytics and back-office operations.
MLS Policy Statement 8.7.

sections.title: NAR IDX Policy and Display Rules for 2026
sections.contentHtml:
The IDX policy governs how marketplace platforms display listings from other MLS participants. The 2026 updates emphasize broker attribution, consumer clarity, and the absolute prohibition of certain data types. Feeds are now delivered exclusively via the RESO Web API.

IDX Access and Feed Management

Access to an IDX feed requires a valid agreement between the MLS, the participant (broker), and the technology vendor (the marketplace platform). As of January 2026, NAR mandates that MLSs act on requests for IDX feeds within five (5) business days, barring extenuating circumstances. The "actual and apparent control" rule remains in effect.

Core Display and Attribution Requirements

Every property detail page (PDP) and thumbnail display must include:

* The listing brokerage firm name in a reasonably prominent location.

* The listing broker's email address OR phone number as provided by the listing participant.

* These fields must be automated in UI templates — manual entry is not sufficient for compliance.

Display Element2026 Requirement
Implementation NoteBroker Attribution
Firm Name + Phone OR EmailMust be 'reasonably prominent' on every PDP and thumbnail.
Buyer Broker CompensationPROHIBITED — Absolute ban
Filter ALL remark fields, note fields, and any free-text areas.Seller Concessions
PermittedDisplay ONLY from designated concession fields in the MLS feed.
Listing Status Purge24-Hour Removal
Expired and Withdrawn listings must be removed within 24 hours.Photo Watermarks
Must be preservedDo not crop, overlay, or remove watermarks from listing photos.
Co-mingling (MLS + Non-MLS)Label or separate
FSBO / non-MLS data must be labeled or shown separately unless local MLS has dropped restriction. 

Compensation and Concession Display — Post-Sitzer/Burnett

A critical mandate arising from the August 17, 2024, Sitzer/Burnett settlement is the absolute prohibition of buyer broker compensation offers within the MLS and its data feeds. Marketplace platforms are strictly forbidden from using MLS data to create or maintain any platform that aggregates compensation offers. Violating this rule is a material breach of the data license resulting in immediate termination of all MLS access.

CRITICAL: The BuyerAgencyCompensation field has been deprecated and removed from the RESO Data Dictionary following the settlement. Any feed ingestion engine referencing this field must be updated to treat it as a deprecated/null field.

sections.title: NAR VOW Policy: Virtual Office Website Compliance
sections.contentHtml:
The Virtual Office Website (VOW) is the primary vehicle for marketplaces providing enhanced data services to registered clients. VOW compliance is more rigorous than IDX, requiring a documented consumer-broker relationship before rich data can be accessed.

Registration and User Authentication

Every VOW must implement a registration wall that:

* Captures the consumer's full name and a valid email address.

* Sends a confirmation email and verifies the consumer's address before granting access.

* Requires the consumer to affirmatively accept a Terms of Use (TOU) restricting data to personal, non-commercial use.

* Prohibits redistribution or retransmission of any data.

* Maintains audit logs of registrations and listings viewed by each user to facilitate MLS compliance reviews.

Rich Data and Non-Disclosure State Constraints

VOW feeds provide access to historical sold data essential for valuation tools. As of 2026, twelve (12) states do not require public recording of real estate sale prices. VOW displays in these areas must strictly adhere to local MLS rules.

StateDisclosure Status
Key Operational ConstraintAlaska
Full Non-DisclosureSold prices are confidential; rely on agent-led research.
IdahoFull Non-Disclosure
Prices not publicly recorded; AVMs must label outputs as 'estimates'.Kansas
Full Non-DisclosureMarket research requires MLS access via professional boards.
LouisianaFull Non-Disclosure
Deep legal roots in transaction privacy; prices withheld from public record.Mississippi
Full Non-DisclosureDeeds list token consideration only (e.g., $10); no public price data available.
MissouriPartial Non-Disclosure
Major counties (St. Louis City, St. Louis County, Jackson County / Kansas City metro) disclose; remaining counties remain private. Verify per-county.Montana
ConfidentialSellers file Realty Transfer Certificates; clerk keeps the information private.
New MexicoFull Non-Disclosure
Strictly non-disclosure; prices only disclosed to owners and licensed agents.North Dakota
Full Non-DisclosureEngaging a realtor with MLS access is the only route for research.
TexasFull Non-Disclosure
Prices never public; AVMs must use VOW-sourced historical data only.Utah
Strict Non-DisclosureOmits prices from all public records; among the highest privacy protections nationally.
WyomingFull Non-Disclosure
Sales information accessible via professional MLS only.

sections.title: Clear Cooperation Policy (CCP) and Listing Status
sections.contentHtml:
The Clear Cooperation Policy (CCP) requires that any listing publicly marketed must be submitted to the MLS within one (1) business day. In 2026, CCP operates alongside the Multiple Listing Options for Sellers (MLOS) framework, which introduced the Delayed Marketing Exempt Listing (DMEL) category.

The DMEL Framework

A DMEL is a listing where the seller has authorized the listing agent to withhold the listing from IDX feeds and syndication for a delay period set by the local MLS. During the delay, the listing is active and visible to all MLS participants and their registered clients but cannot appear on public-facing portals.

* Listing agents must obtain a signed Exempt Listing Disclosure (seller consent form) — exact form name varies by MLS.

* CRMLS does not support the DMEL category (see Section 6.1).

* Stellar MLS requires the Owners Exclusion Form be uploaded within 24 hours of an MLS request.

Public Marketing Triggers

The following events constitute public marketing and trigger the one business day CCP submission requirement:

* Yard signs or physical marketing materials.

* Digital marketing on any public website, including social media posts.

* Emails sent to more than one (1) outside brokerage.

CLARIFICATION: NAR has confirmed that one-to-one communications between brokers do NOT trigger CCP. Multi-brokerage communications DO trigger CCP.

Zillow Listing Access Standards (LAS) — Corrected

Zillow and Trulia implemented their Listing Access Standards (LAS) requiring that any publicly marketed property appear in the MLS within one business day to be eligible for display on their sites. Key corrected dates:

* LAS announced: April 2025.

* LAS enforcement began: June 30, 2025 (not 'mid-2025' as previously stated).

* Preliminary injunction against LAS denied: February 6, 2026 (Compass v. Zillow).

* If a listing is marketed for more than 24 hours before MLS entry, Zillow may ban that listing for the life of the listing agreement.

LEGAL NOTE: The February 6, 2026 Compass v. Zillow ruling ONLY denied Compass's motion for a preliminary injunction. It is NOT a final judgment on the merits. The underlying antitrust litigation is ONGOING in the Eastern District. Do not characterize this ruling as confirming the 'legality' of Zillow's LAS. Compliance files should note case status as 'active litigation, preliminary injunction denied.'

Listing Status IDX/VOW Reference

StatusIDX Inclusion
VOW InclusionCompliance Implication
ActiveYes
YesStandard listing status; full distribution.
Coming SoonNo (Local Option)
YesRestricted public display; time-limited per local MLS.
DMELNo
YesGated display only during delay period; requires seller consent form.
Office ExclusiveNo
NoNo public marketing allowed; internal participant use only.
PendingLocal Option
YesMust check local MLS per-feed settings before display.
ExpiredNo
NoMust be purged from all displays within 24 hours of expiration.
WithdrawnNo
NoMust be purged from all displays within 24 hours.

sections.title: RESO Web API Technical Standards
sections.contentHtml:
The technical architecture of the U.S. real estate market is now built exclusively on the RESO Web API standard. The legacy Real Estate Transaction Standard (RETS) is fully deprecated and must not be used in any 2026 integration.

Data Dictionary 2.0 Certification

NAR-affiliated MLSs were required to achieve RESO Data Dictionary 2.0 certification by April 2025. Significant updates include standardized status values (Active, Pending, Closed), detailed environmental hazard fields (fire/flood risk), and transition to 'Primary' terminology for property features.

UPCOMING: RESO is moving toward Data Dictionary 2.1 and 2.2 releases, which introduce fields for digital staging and AI-enhanced photo disclosure including MediaDigitalEnhancementYN (see Section 11).

Deprecated and New Fields — 2026

FieldStatus
Action RequiredBuyerAgencyCompensation
DEPRECATED — Removed post-settlementRemove from ingestion schema; treat as null.
AutomatedValuationModelYNActive — Correct spelling
Respect opt-out flag per property.MediaDigitalEnhancementYN
New in DD 2.2Flag AI-staged photos on display; show side-by-side with original.
OriginalMediaUrlNew / Updated
Provides non-altered source images; required by emerging consumer protection laws.ListingKeyNumeric
ActiveUse as primary unique identifier for listings across feeds.

Technical Specifications

Specification2026 Requirement
Technical StandardTransport Protocol
RESTful HTTPRESO Web API.
Query LanguageOData v4
Filters, Paging, and Ordering.Response Format
JSONOptimized for mobile and web delivery.
Security LayerTLS 1.2 minimum
TLS 1.3 strongly recommended.Auth Method
OAuth 2.0Bearer Tokens and Scopes.
NotificationWebhooks / EntityEvent
Real-time push updates.Legacy Protocol
RETS — PROHIBITEDReject any MLS still on RETS; flag for escalation.

sections.title: Regional MLS Compliance — Major Organizations
sections.contentHtml:
National platforms must reconcile the NAR baseline with the specific, often more aggressive, compliance regimes of large regional MLS organizations. The 2026 'local discretion' framework has significantly increased per-MLS variability.

CRMLS — California Regional MLS (Largest U.S. MLS)

CRMLS covers the bulk of California's residential real estate market and is the largest MLS in the United States.

* CRMLS does NOT support the DMEL category. Any listing publicly marketed must be entered within one business day and is immediately eligible for IDX and VOW distribution.

* Strict no-commingling rule: MLS-sourced data and non-MLS data must be displayed in separate sections or clearly labeled per listing.

Bright MLS — Mid-Atlantic (DC, MD, VA, PA, NJ, DE, WV)

* Bright has removed previous 'Coming Soon' status time limits, giving sellers more control over showing availability dates.

* Requires separate signed agreements for IDX data and VOW data access.

* Conducts automated audits of website displays to verify broker attribution (firm name + phone/email) on every property detail page.

* Co-mingling restrictions apply; verify current Bright MLS IDX agreement for 2026 status.

Stellar MLS — Florida and Puerto Rico

* Implemented the DMEL category on September 16, 2025.

* Agents must upload a signed Owners Exclusion Form within 24 hours of an MLS request to verify the delay period.

* Strong 'photo integrity' enforcement: severe fines for any platform that crops watermarks or uses listing photos without the listing firm's explicit license.

REBNY / OneKey MLS — New York City and Metro New York

REBNY (Real Estate Board of New York) is NOT NAR-affiliated and operates under its own distinct framework. OneKey MLS serves the broader downstate New York region.

* REBNY has its own IDX rules separate from NAR Policy 7.58.

* Exclusive listings and office exclusives play a larger role in NYC than in any other U.S. market.

* Platforms operating in NYC must execute REBNY-specific data license agreements, not standard NAR IDX agreements.

* OneKey MLS serves Long Island, Hudson Valley, and surrounding counties; standard NAR IDX rules apply to OneKey.

NWMLS — Northwest MLS (Washington State)

NWMLS is NOT NAR-affiliated and operates under its own independently governed rules.

* No mandatory buyer compensation sharing was ever required by NWMLS — the Sitzer/Burnett settlement had limited direct impact on NWMLS operations.

* IDX and data sharing are governed entirely by NWMLS rules, not NAR Policy 7.58.

* Platforms must execute NWMLS-specific participant agreements.

REcolorado — Denver Metro and Colorado

* REcolorado implemented DMEL in 2025; verify current delay period allowance.

* Standard NAR IDX rules apply; RESO Web API fully deployed.

* CCPA-equivalent Colorado Privacy Act (CPA) applies; honor universal opt-out signal.

ARMLS — Arizona Regional MLS (Phoenix Metro)

* Standard NAR IDX rules apply.

* Arizona was one of five state AGs that co-filed the FTC complaint against Zillow/Redfin (filed Oct 1, 2025).

* ARMLS is a high-volume market; platforms should verify DMEL implementation status.

ABOR — Austin Board of REALTORS (Texas)

* Texas is a full non-disclosure state. All AVM features on ABOR-sourced data must use VOW-sourced historical records only.

* Public price data does not exist in Texas; do not surface sold prices on IDX displays.

* AVMs must be labeled as estimates at all times.

Canopy MLS — Carolinas (NC / SC)

* Canopy MLS is among the largest MLSs in the Southeast.

* Co-mingling restrictions: verify current 2026 status in Canopy MLS IDX agreement.

* North Carolina and South Carolina are disclosure states; full sold price data available via VOW.

Miami REALTORS MLS — Southeast Florida

* Miami REALTORS has specific rules on the display of international listings and buyer concessions that reflect the unique demographics of the South Florida market.

* Florida is a Thompson Broker state (see Section 1.3); non-NAR licensed brokers retain MLS access rights.

* Verify current 2026 co-mingling and DMEL status with Miami REALTORS compliance team.

sections.title: Privacy Law Compliance and the HPPA
sections.contentHtml:
Homebuyers Privacy Protection Act (HPPA) — Corrected Date

The Homebuyers Privacy Protection Act (H.R. 2808) amends the Fair Credit Reporting Act. It largely bans the sale of mortgage 'trigger leads'.

CRITICAL DATE: The HPPA effective date is March 5, 2026.Credit reporting agencies are prohibited from furnishing a consumer report in connection with a residential mortgage transaction to any third party unless that party has the consumer's documented opt-in consent or holds an existing relationship with the consumer.

* Real estate marketplaces partnering with lenders must completely overhaul lead-generation workflows to ensure all consumer information shared with a lender is preceded by a clear and documented opt-in.

CCPA, GPC, and ADMT — California

* Effective January 1, 2026, platforms must honor the Global Privacy Control (GPC) signal as a valid opt-out request for the sale or sharing of personal information.

* ADMT regulations take effect April 1, 2027. Platforms using AI for listing ranking, AVM estimates, or consumer matching must provide a pre-use notice and allow consumers to opt out.

State Privacy Law Matrix — 2026

StateLaw
2026 StatusKey Marketplace Requirement
CaliforniaCCPA / CPRA
Updated Jan 2026Honor GPC signal; ADMT pre-use notice by April 1, 2027.
VirginiaVCDPA
ActiveRight to opt out of targeted advertising; data subject rights.
ColoradoCPA
ActiveUniversal opt-out signal recognition; data processor obligations.
TexasTDPSA
ActiveDetailed data processor obligations; controller accountability.
FloridaFDBR
ActiveController and processor accountability requirements.
ConnecticutCTDPA
ActiveConsumer rights: access, deletion, portability, correction.
WashingtonMy Health MY Data Act
ActiveBroad health data protections; relevant to AVM and property condition data.

sections.title: CAN-SPAM and Email Marketing Compliance
sections.contentHtml:
The FTC's CAN-SPAM Act remains the federal standard for commercial email. Unlike European (GDPR) or Canadian (CASL) standards, CAN-SPAM does not require prior opt-in for commercial messages but imposes strict formatting and operational requirements.

Primary Purpose Test and Penalties

* Every automated listing alert, newsletter, or follow-up email must be evaluated against the 'Primary Purpose' test.

* If a reasonable recipient interpreting the subject line would conclude the message promotes a commercial product or service, all CAN-SPAM rules apply.

* Transactional messages (e.g., VOW registration confirmations) are largely exempt, provided they contain no false or misleading routing information.

* Each separate email in violation of CAN-SPAM is subject to civil penalties of up to $53,088 (2026 FTC-adjusted figure).

* Unsubscribe mechanisms must be clearly visible and all opt-out requests honored within ten (10) business days.

sections.title: FinCEN Residential Real Estate Rule — Effective March 1, 2026
sections.contentHtml:
The Financial Crimes Enforcement Network (FinCEN), a bureau of the U.S. Treasury Department, implemented a nationwide Residential Real Estate (RRE) Rule on March 1, 2026. This rule replaces the prior system of Geographic Targeting Orders (GTOs), which applied only in select cities with purchase price thresholds. The new rule has no geographic limitations and no minimum purchase price — it applies nationwide to any qualifying transaction.

SCOPE: The RRE Rule applies when all three conditions are met: (1) residential real estate is transferred, (2) the buyer is a legal entity or trust — NOT an individual taking title in their own name, and (3) the transaction is non-financed, meaning no mortgage from a regulated bank secured by the property.

What Is a 'Non-Financed' Transfer

A transfer is non-financed unless it involves credit that is both secured by the property being transferred AND extended by a financial institution subject to federal AML program and SAR requirements. The following all qualify as non-financed and are therefore reportable if the buyer is an entity or trust: all-cash purchases, seller-financed transactions, privately financed transactions, and loans from non-bank lenders not subject to AML obligations.

Covered Property Types

* Single-family houses, townhouses, condominiums, and cooperatives (including units in large multi-unit buildings)

* One-to-four family residential properties

* Vacant land where the purchaser intends to construct a one-to-four family residential structure

* Mixed-use and apartment buildings

* Applies in all 50 states, Washington D.C., Puerto Rico, and Native American lands

Who Must File — Reporting Person Cascade

The filing obligation falls on designated 'reporting persons' involved in the closing or settlement — not on buyers or sellers directly. The cascade works as follows: the first person on a defined list who is involved in the transaction must file. The list includes: the closing or settlement agent that prepares the closing statement, the title insurance company, the escrow company, the buyer's attorney, and the seller's attorney, in that priority order.

ATTORNEY STATES: Attorneys in states where attorneys routinely handle escrow and title — including New York, Connecticut, Delaware, Georgia, Massachusetts, North Carolina, South Carolina, Vermont, and West Virginia — should expect to be designated as the reporting person in many transactions.

What Must Be Reported

* Property address and legal description

* Closing date and purchase price

* Payment method

* For entity buyers: full name, address, jurisdiction of formation, EIN, and beneficial owner information for all individuals with 25%+ ownership or substantial control

* For trust buyers: trustee information, grantor information, beneficiary information, and information on any individuals with authority to control decisions on behalf of the trust

Filing Deadline and Retention

* Report must be filed by the LATER of: (1) the last day of the month following the month of closing, OR (2) 30 calendar days after closing date.

* The reporting person must retain a copy of the filed report for five years.

* FinCEN does not permit submission of reports with missing required information — collect all data before closing.

Exemptions

* Transfers occurring by reason of death or inheritance

* Transfers incident to divorce or dissolution of marriage

* Court-ordered transfers

* Bankruptcy proceedings

* Certain Section 1031 exchange transactions (narrowly defined — verify on a per-transaction basis)

NOT EXEMPT: Individual buyers taking title in their own name are not covered (no reporting required regardless of payment method). But the moment an LLC, partnership, corporation, or trust is the buyer, the rule applies to non-financed transactions.

ScenarioReportable?
ReasonIndividual buys home with all cash, takes title in own name
NOBuyer is an individual, not entity or trust
LLC buys home with all cashYES
Entity buyer + non-financedTrust buys home, no mortgage
YESTrust buyer + non-financed
LLC buys home with regulated bank mortgageNO
Financed by AML-regulated institutionLLC buys home with seller financing
YESSeller financing = non-financed under rule
Individual buys home, places in LLC after closingNO
Transfer at closing was to individual

sections.title: Washington State SB 6091 — Pocket Listing Prohibition
sections.contentHtml:
Washington State Senate Bill 6091 was signed into law by Governor Bob Ferguson in mid-March 2026, following unanimous passage in the Senate (49-0 on February 10, 2026) and passage through the House. The law adds a new provision to Chapter 18.86 RCW (Washington's real estate brokerage law) prohibiting selective, exclusive, or private listing marketing for residential real estate.

EFFECTIVE DATE — CRITICAL: SB 6091 was signed in March 2026 but does NOT take immediate effect. The law goes into effect approximately three months after signing — June 10, 2026. Platforms should prepare for enforcement from that date, not from the signing date.

What the Law Prohibits

Under SB 6091, a Washington-licensed broker may NOT market the sale or lease of residential real estate to a limited or exclusive group of prospective buyers or brokers, or any combination thereof, unless the real estate is concurrently marketed to the general public and all other brokers.

* Prohibition: marketing to selective, private, or exclusive buyer networks without simultaneous public marketing

* Required: any residential listing a broker markets must be accessible to all consumers and all Washington-licensed brokers at the same time

* Enforcement: violations are subject to disciplinary action by the Washington State Department of Licensing

What the Law Does NOT Prohibit

* Private party sales: a seller may sell their property privately without listing it — the law only applies once a broker markets the property

* Not an MLS mandate: the law does NOT require MLS entry. Public marketing can be as simple as posting on the broker's own website

* Technology neutral: the law does not specify how public marketing must occur — no sign, open house, or MLS listing required, only that the listing be broadly accessible

* Health/safety exception: exclusive marketing is permitted where reasonably necessary to protect the health or safety of the owner or occupant

Platform Compliance Implications

* Washington platforms and brokers using private listing network models (e.g. Compass Private Exclusives) must redesign workflows before the effective date

* NWMLS already prohibited private listings — SB 6091 creates a uniform statewide standard superseding the patchwork of MLS-specific rules

* The law applies to all Washington-licensed brokers regardless of which MLS they belong to, closing the previous gap where non-NWMLS brokers could use private listing strategies

* Platforms should update their listing intake and compliance screening for Washington-state listings by June 2026

NATIONAL SIGNIFICANCE: Washington is the first state to codify a pocket listing prohibition into broker licensing law. Industry observers expect other states to introduce similar legislation in 2026-2027. Oregon, Colorado, and Minnesota have been cited as likely next movers.

sections.title: NYC FARE Act — Broker Fee and Disclosure Rules (Effective June 11, 2025)
sections.contentHtml:
FRAMING CORRECTION: The FARE Act is sometimes described as 'REBNY rental requirements.' This is factually wrong. REBNY (Real Estate Board of New York) actively challenged the FARE Act in court and lost. The FARE Act is New York City Local Law 119 of 2024, passed by the NYC Council. It has been in effect since June 11, 2025.

The Fairness in Apartment Rental Expenses (FARE) Act took effect on June 11, 2025, fundamentally changing how broker fees are handled in New York City apartment rentals. It is enforced by the NYC Department of Consumer and Worker Protection (DCWP). Any real estate marketplace platform displaying NYC rental listings must comply with the FARE Act's advertising disclosure requirements.

Core Rule — Fee Follows Representation

* If a broker represents the landlord (including any listing agent who publishes a listing with the landlord's permission), the LANDLORD pays the broker fee — not the tenant.

* If a tenant independently hires their own broker, the TENANT pays that broker's fee.

* Landlords cannot require tenants to hire a specific broker, including a dual agent, as a condition of renting.

* There is a rebuttable presumption that any broker who publishes a listing did so with the landlord's permission — making the landlord potentially liable for that broker's actions.

Mandatory Listing Disclosure Requirements for Platforms

This section directly governs marketplace platform listing display obligations for NYC rental listings:

* All fees that prospective tenants must pay to rent an apartment must be conspicuously disclosed in every advertisement or listing — including application fees, credit check fees, and any other fees payable before lease signing.

* Listings may NOT include broker fees payable by the tenant when the broker represents the landlord.

* Platforms displaying NYC rental listings must not accept or display listings that pass illegal broker fees to tenants.

* Before lease signing, landlords or their agents must provide tenants a signed, itemized written disclosure of ALL fees — the signed disclosure must be retained for three years and a copy given to the tenant.

Permitted Fees

* Background and credit check fees: permitted up to $20 total per applicant (New York State law cap).

* Tenant-hired broker fees: fully permitted — if the tenant initiates the broker relationship and pays the fee directly.

* Security deposits: capped at one month's rent under New York State law.

Enforcement and Penalties

* Enforcing body: NYC DCWP — issues summonses and pursues penalties through OATH hearings.

* Civil penalties: up to $2,000 per violation (escalating schedule for repeat violations).

* Tenants have a private right of action — they can sue in civil court to recover illegally charged fees.

* Landlords are liable for violations committed by their agents, even if the landlord had no direct contact with the tenant.

* REBNY's constitutional challenge and request for a preliminary injunction were both denied — the law is fully enforceable.

sections.title: Fair Housing and ADA Web Accessibility
sections.contentHtml:
Algorithmic Bias and Disparate Impact

* HUD's 2023 guidance on AI and fair housing confirmed the 'disparate impact' standard applies to algorithmic tools used by real estate platforms.

* Platforms must regularly audit search-ranking algorithms and recommendation engines to ensure they do not systematically steer users based on protected characteristics.

* Use of geographic proxies (ZIP code, school district) in ranking models requires rigorous testing for discriminatory outcomes.

WCAG 2.1 Level AA Digital Accessibility

* Federal courts have consistently held WCAG 2.1 Level AA as the compliance standard for real estate websites.

* Descriptive alt-text required on all property photos (e.g., 'Kitchen with granite countertops and stainless steel appliances').

* Keyboard-only operability required for all interactive maps.

* Skip-navigation links required for screen readers.

* At least one manual screen-reader audit must be performed annually.

sections.title: AI, AVM, and Digital Staging Compliance
sections.contentHtml:
AVM Data Sourcing Constraints

* Training an AVM requires historical sold data. In non-disclosure states, this must be sourced exclusively via authorized VOW feeds.

* Platforms are strictly prohibited from scraping sold data from county websites or competitor portals for AVM training.

* Sellers may opt out of AVM display using the AutomatedValuationModelYN flag in the MLS feed Platforms must respect the opt-out flag per individual property on every page render.

AI-Enhanced Photo Disclosure

* The 2026 compliance standard requires clear labeling of virtually staged or AI-enhanced photos.

* RESO Data Dictionary 2.2 introduces the MediaDigitalEnhancementYN field to automate this disclosure.

* Marketplace displays should provide a side-by-side or alternating view of original and AI-staged photos.

* The OriginalMediaUrl flag in the RESO feed provides access to the non-altered source image.

sections.title: Antitrust and Competition Law — 2026
sections.contentHtml:
FTC / State AG Action Against Zillow and Redfin

The FTC filed suit on September 30, 2025, against Zillow and Redfin centering on a multifamily rental syndication agreement. The complaint alleges the $100 million deal effectively eliminated Redfin as a competitor in the ILS advertising market for large unit complexes.

Compass v. Zillow — Case Status

Antitrust Risk AreaCompliance Strategy
Regulatory FocusExclusive Data Deals
Ensure non-exclusive access to all MLS feeds.FTC Bureau of Competition.
Compensation AggregationDo not display or host any broker compensation data.
NAR Settlement Mandate.Algorithmic Pricing
Use independent pricing models; avoid coordination.Sherman Act Section 1.
Listing BansNeutral, consistent application of transparency rules.
Compass v. Zillow (active case).State AG Risk
Monitor AZ, CT, NY, VA, WA enforcement actions.Five-state AG coalition (Oct 2025).

AnnualVendor SPA
Signed CCPA Service Provider Agreements for all processors.At Onboarding
Thompson BrokerVerify non-NAR broker access rights in FL, GA, AL.
On Feed Setup 

COMPLIANCE LOG: Platforms must maintain a Compliance Log detailing every automated status purge, registration verification, and audit result. This log is essential for defending against MLS violation notices or regulatory inquiries. Respond to any MLS violation notice within 24 hours with a written remediation plan.
